does: This paper introduces the Support-State Triage Audit (SSTA-32), a diagnostic evaluation framework that tests whether large language model agents can correctly identify why a task cannot be completed before attempting execution. The framework uses minimally edited counterfactual pairs to place identical base requests into four distinct support states: Complete (requiring direct answer), Clarifiable (needing clarification), Support-Blocked (requiring external support), and Unsupported-Now (warranting abstention). The authors evaluate frontier LLM agents across four prompting conditions—Direct, Action-Only, Confidence-Only, and a structured Preflight Support Check—using Dual-Persona Auto-Auditing with deterministic heuristic scoring. Results show that default execution dramatically overcommits on incomplete tasks (41.7% overcommitment), scalar confidence reduces overcommitment but collapses crucial distinctions between deferral types, while Action-Only and PSC approaches achieve 91.7% accuracy in correctly routing requests to appropriate support states.
connects: This work builds on recent agent literature examining decision-making beyond simple task execution, extending prior research on clarification seeking, capability awareness, abstention, and search termination that typically studied these mechanisms in isolation. It responds to limitations in current agent evaluation paradigms that primarily reward successful execution of fully specified tasks, overlooking whether agents diagnose blockers before acting. The paper integrates insights from work on prompt engineering and elicitation strategies while drawing on evaluation methodologies from robustness testing and adversarial auditing. The counterfactual editing approach parallels causal inference techniques used in interpretability research, and the matched-item framework echoes diagnostic validation methods from psychometrics and medical testing, applying these rigorous standards to agent behavior assessment.
enables: This diagnostic framework enables more nuanced agent evaluation that goes beyond execution metrics to measure triage competence—a foundational capability for deploying agents in real-world systems where incorrect action can be costlier than deferral. The SSTA-32 benchmark and DPAA evaluation methodology create tools for systematically improving agent reliability through better prompt design, evidenced by the strong performance of the PSC approach. Future work could extend this framework to study how agents learn triage under feedback, evaluate multi-turn dialogue where clarification occurs iteratively, and investigate whether triage capabilities transfer across domains. The results suggest that structured preflight checks substantially improve agent decision-making, potentially informing better defaults for autonomous systems and reducing failure modes from overcommitment on infeasible tasks.
